entries.title: Prompt - Spring AI and Ollama API

contents: This example demonstrates how to prompt a locally hosted large language model
(LLM) using [Ollama](https://ollama.com/). Ollama is an open-source platform and
command-line tool that enables developers to download, manage, and run LLMs
entirely on their own machines. This removes the need for cloud APIs, access
keys, and many privacy concerns. It supports multimodal inputs, tool calling,
and integration via REST and
[Java](https://docs.spring.io/spring-ai/reference/api/chat/ollama-chat.html),
making it well suited to prototyping, debugging, and deploying LLM-powered
applications in a fully offline and customisable environment.
This example uses [Spring AI](https://spring.io/projects/spring-ai), a
Java-based framework for building applications powered by LLMs. It provides
support for prompt management, chaining LLM calls, memory handling,
retrieval-augmented generation (RAG), and integration with external data
sources, all within the Java ecosystem.
[LangChain4J](https://github.com/langchain4j/langchain4j) is an alternative
framework that also offers an abstraction layer between LLMs and the
application.
The examples provided in this repository are for **educational purposes only**.
While every effort has been made to ensure accuracy and reliability, these
examples are provided **“as is”**, without any warranties, express or implied.
By using these examples, you acknowledge that you do so **at your own risk**.
The authors and contributors shall **not be held liable** for any direct,
indirect, incidental, or consequential damages resulting from the use, misuse,
or inability to use these examples.
It is the responsibility of the user to **review, test, and validate** any code
before applying it in a production or commercial environment.
- [Oracle Java 25](https://www.oracle.com/java/technologies/downloads/#java25)
- [Ollama](https://ollama.com/download)
- [curl](https://curl.se/)
- [jq](https://jqlang.org/)
1. Download and setup Ollama
If you have not already done so, download and install Ollama from the
[official download page](https://ollama.com/download), following the provided
instructions. Ollama includes a command-line interface (CLI) for managing and
interacting with models.
Models can be downloaded from repositories such as the
[Ollama Library](https://ollama.com/library) or
[Hugging Face](https://huggingface.co/models). Ensure the model is available
in a supported format such as
[GGUF (GPT-Generated Unified Format)](https://huggingface.co/docs/hub/en/gguf).
To list models installed locally, run:

output.caption: After a fresh installation, no models will be listed.

contents: To get started, download [LLaMA 2](https://ollama.com/library/llama2) with:

caption: This process may take a few seconds to several minutes, depending on your
internet connection.

contents: Once the model is downloaded, you can begin using it.
(Optional) To confirm the model has been installed, list the models again:

output.caption: You should now see `llama2` in the list.

contents: To interact with the model using the command-line interface, run:
```shell
ollama run llama2
This opens a prompt where you can engage with the model. If the model has not
yet been downloaded, Ollama will download it automatically. You can interact
with the model via the CLI
>>> When did humans first land on the Moon?

Humans first landed on the Moon on July 20, 1969, during the Apollo 11 mission. The spacecraft carrying the astronauts, Neil Armstrong and Edwin "Buzz" Aldrin, landed on the lunar surface in the Sea of Tranquility at 20:17 UTC (Coordinated Universal Time). Armstrong became the first person to set foot on the Moon, famously declaring "That's one small step for man, one giant leap for mankind" as he stepped out of the lunar module Eagle.
>>> /bye
Alternatively, you can interact with the model through a graphical interface,
such as [Open WebUI](https://github.com/open-webui/open-webui), which offers
a web-based experience similar to cloud-hosted models.
To verify that Ollama is running and accessible on the default port `11434`,
use:

output.caption: This will return a list of available models.

contents: When using Ollama via the REST API, as in the following steps, the required
model must be downloaded in advance. Ollama does not automatically download
missing models when accessed through the REST API; this functionality is only
available via the CLI, for example with `ollama run llama2`. Please ensure
the `llama2` model is available before proceeding.
Verify that the Ollama APIs can be successfully invoked.

commands: "content": "When did humans first land on the Moon?"

contents: 2. Build the application
This step assumes that Ollama is accessible on port `11434` and that the
`llama2` model is available. For further details, please refer to Step 1.
(_Optional_) Verify that the application was created.
3. Run the program
This step assumes that Ollama is accessible on port `11434` and that the
`llama2` model is available. For further details, please refer to Step 1.
Run the example with the default prompt.

caption: Although the prompt remains the same, the output may vary with each run.

contents: Run the example again with specific prompt.

commands: 'Who was the first person to walk on the Moon?'

caption: The model should respond with 'Neil Armstrong', as he was the first person to
walk on the Moon.

contents: Should you require any assistance in using this workshop or have any feedback,
please reach out to Albert Attard (`albert.attard@oracle.com`).
